## [00:00 - 03:02]

There are four stages of learning and most people never get to the final and fourth stage of learning. Stage number one, that is unconscious incompetence. What that essentially means is you don't know what you don't know and that's stage one of learning. And generally that's where you are before you've joined a communication skills class. Maybe before you've joined my class, you're in stage one of learning. You're unconsciously incompetent. You don't know what you don't know. You don't know what you're not good at. You don't know what you're good at. Okay, so that's where you are in stage one. Then as you start the learning journey, as you join one of my classes, as you start consuming some of my content, then what happens is you move to stage two of learning, which is conscious incompetence. What that means now is you're aware of the things you are good at and you're starting to build an awareness about the things you're not good at, right? Because now you're consciously incompetent. Then after that, you move to stage three as you start to practice the things you learn. You start to apply the knowledge that you've acquired, which is conscious competence. And conscious competence, that's when you still have to think about it, but you're doing it, you're doing it, but you still got to think about it. You're still thinking about the hand gestures. You're still thinking about the way you're manipulating your voice while you're doing it. However, you're on the way towards mastery. And then as you stick to the stage three of learning, as you continue to review and reflect on your delivery, as you continue to plan your improvement, as you continue to take your skills to the next level, you finally arrive at stage four. And stage four is unconscious competence. This is a beautiful state of mastery. And there are many areas in your life where you have already achieved stage four of mastery, one of which is driving. For example, when you first learned how to drive the car, do you remember how difficult it was? I don't know for you, but for me, it was so crazy. I remember I had to get in the car, sit down.
